questions.q: Which equation is NOT part of the definition of a category?
choices: $g\circ f = f\circ g$ whenever both composites are defined
questions.explain: Composition in a category is associative and unital, but not commutative. In $\mathsf{Set}$ already, $g\circ f\ne f\circ g$ in general.

questions.q: In a category $\mathcal{C}$, an isomorphism $f\colon A\to B$ is a morphism such that:
choices: $f$ is a bijection on underlying sets
there exists $g\colon B\to A$ with $g\circ f=\mathrm{id}_A$ and $f\circ g=\mathrm{id}_B$
questions.explain: Isomorphism is a categorical notion: a morphism with a two-sided inverse. Only in $\mathsf{Set}$ does this specialize to bijection.

questions.q: How many morphisms does the category $\mathbf{2}=(\bullet\xrightarrow{f}\bullet)$ with two objects and one non-identity arrow have in total (including identities)?
questions.explain: Two identities $\mathrm{id}_0,\mathrm{id}_1$ plus the single arrow $f$ gives $3$ morphisms.

hard.q: A morphism $f\colon A\to B$ in a category is a monomorphism if and only if:
choices: for all $g,h\colon C\to A$, $f\circ g = f\circ h$ implies $g=h$
for all $g,h\colon B\to C$, $g\circ f = h\circ f$ implies $g=h$
hard.explain: A monomorphism is left-cancellable: $f\circ g=f\circ h\Rightarrow g=h$. This is the categorical generalisation of injectivity. Having a left inverse implies mono, but not conversely — e.g. inclusions in $\mathsf{Top}$ can be mono without being split.

hard.q: Consider the category $\mathcal{B}G$ associated to a group $G$ (one object $*$, morphisms = elements of $G$, composition = multiplication). A functor $F\colon\mathcal{B}G\to\mathsf{Set}$ is the same as:
hard.explain: $F$ must send $*$ to some set $S=F(*)$ and each $g\in G$ to a function $S\to S$, preserving composition and identity. This is precisely the data of a left $G$-set (group action on $S$).

hard.q: The category of a poset $(P,\le)$ has a morphism $x\to y$ iff $x\le y$. In the poset $\{0<1<2\}$, how many morphisms (including identities) are there in total?
hard.explain: Identities: $0\to 0$, $1\to 1$, $2\to 2$ (3 morphisms). Non-identity morphisms: $0\to 1$, $1\to 2$, $0\to 2$ (3 morphisms). Total: $6$.

questions.q: A functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$ must satisfy which property on composition?
choices: $F$ need not respect composition — only objects
questions.explain: Covariant functors preserve the order of composition: $F(g\circ f)=F(g)\circ F(f)$. Contravariant functors reverse it — those are functors out of $\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}$.

questions.q: A natural transformation $\eta\colon F\Rightarrow G$ between $F,G\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$ consists of components $\eta_A\colon F(A)\to G(A)$ satisfying, for every $f\colon A\to B$ in $\mathcal{C}$:
questions.explain: The naturality square: $G(f)\circ\eta_A=\eta_B\circ F(f)$. The transformation commutes with the action of $f$ on both sides.

questions.q: How many functors are there from the terminal category $\mathbf{1}$ (one object, only identity) to a category $\mathcal{C}$ with exactly $5$ objects?
questions.explain: A functor $\mathbf{1}\to\mathcal{C}$ just picks an object of $\mathcal{C}$, so the count is $|\mathrm{Ob}(\mathcal{C})|=5$.

hard.q: The evaluation map $\mathrm{ev}_V\colon V\to V^{**}$, $v\mapsto(\varphi\mapsto\varphi(v))$, is a natural transformation $\mathrm{id}_{\mathsf{Vect}_k}\Rightarrow(-)^{**}$. Why is there NO natural isomorphism $\mathrm{id}\Rightarrow(-)^*$ (single dual) on all of $\mathsf{Vect}_k$?
choices: $(-)^*$ is a covariant functor, so no natural transformation can exist
$(-)^*$ is contravariant, so its domain and codomain categories differ from those of $\mathrm{id}$
Any isomorphism $V\cong V^*$ requires a choice of basis and cannot be made natural
hard.hint: What direction does $(-)^*$ send a linear map $f\colon V\to W$?
hard.explain: $(-)^*=\Hom(-,k)$ is contravariant: it reverses the direction of linear maps. A natural transformation requires components in the same ambient functor category, which forces both functors to have the same variance. Since $\mathrm{id}$ is covariant and $(-)^*$ is contravariant, no natural transformation — let alone isomorphism — between them can be written down.

hard.q: Arrange the steps to verify that a family $\eta=\{\eta_A\}$ is a natural transformation $F\Rightarrow G$. Which ordering is logically correct?
choices: Fix $f\colon A\to B$; (ii) form $G(f)\circ\eta_A$; (iii) form $\eta_B\circ F(f)$; (iv) show equality
Fix $A$; (ii) define $\eta_A\colon F(A)\to G(A)$; (iii) fix $f\colon A\to B$; (iv) check $G(f)\circ\eta_A=\eta_B\circ F(f)$
Show each $\eta_A$ is an isomorphism; (ii) conclude naturality
Check $\eta$ commutes with identities; (ii) conclude it commutes with all morphisms by induction
hard.explain: One must first define all components $\eta_A$, then verify the naturality square for each morphism $f$. Step (i) fixes an arbitrary morphism; steps (ii–iii) spell out both paths around the square; step (iv) proves them equal using the definitions of $F$, $G$, and $\eta$. Isomorphism is not required for a natural transformation.

hard.q: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be the discrete category on $3$ objects (no non-identity morphisms). How many natural transformations are there from the identity functor $\mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{C}}$ to itself?
hard.explain: A natural transformation $\eta\colon\mathrm{id}\Rightarrow\mathrm{id}$ needs, for each object $A$, a morphism $\eta_A\colon A\to A$. In a discrete category the only morphism $A\to A$ is $\mathrm{id}_A$. So there is exactly $1$ natural transformation (all components forced to be identities).

questions.q: The Yoneda lemma states that for a locally small category $\mathcal{C}$, a functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$, and $X\in\mathcal{C}$, the natural transformations $h_X\Rightarrow F$ are in bijection with:
questions.explain: Yoneda: $\mathrm{Nat}(h_X,F)\cong F(X)$ naturally in $X$ and $F$. Evaluating at $\mathrm{id}_X$ recovers the element, and any $x\in F(X)$ determines the transformation $f\mapsto F(f)(x)$.

questions.q: If $F\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$ is left adjoint to $G\colon\mathcal{D}\to\mathcal{C}$, which is true?
questions.explain: Left adjoints preserve colimits (LAPC); right adjoints preserve limits (RAPL). The adjunction $\Hom(F(-),-)\cong\Hom(-,G(-))$ together with Yoneda makes this immediate.

questions.q: In $\mathsf{Set}$, the product (= categorical limit of the discrete two-object diagram) of $\{a,b,c\}$ and $\{0,1\}$ has how many elements?
questions.explain: The product in $\mathsf{Set}$ is the Cartesian product, so $|A\times B|=|A|\cdot|B|=3\cdot 2=6$.

hard.q: The Yoneda embedding $h_{(-)}\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathsf{Fun}(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}},\mathsf{Set})$ is fully faithful. What does full faithfulness imply about objects of $\mathcal{C}$?
choices: $\mathcal{C}$ embeds as a full subcategory; in particular $X\cong Y$ in $\mathcal{C}$ iff $h_X\cong h_Y$ as functors
The embedding is an equivalence of categories
hard.explain: Full faithfulness gives $\Hom_{\mathcal{C}}(X,Y)\cong\operatorname{Nat}(h_X,h_Y)$ (by Yoneda with $F=h_Y$). In particular $X\cong Y\Leftrightarrow h_X\cong h_Y$: objects are determined up to isomorphism by their functor of points. Not every presheaf need be representable (that would make the embedding an equivalence, which is usually false).

hard.q: For the free–forgetful adjunction $F\dashv U\colon\mathsf{Grp}\to\mathsf{Set}$, the unit $\eta_X\colon X\to UF(X)$ is the insertion-of-generators map. What does the counit $\varepsilon_G\colon FU(G)\to G$ do?
choices: It sends every element of $G$ to the identity
It is the unique group homomorphism extending the identity $\mathrm{id}_G\colon G\to G$ along the insertion $G\hookrightarrow FU(G)$
It is the abelianization map $G\to G/[G,G]$
hard.hint: Apply the universal property of the free group to the identity function on $U(G)$.
hard.explain: The counit $\varepsilon_G$ is the evaluation map: $FU(G)$ is the free group on the underlying set of $G$, and $\varepsilon_G$ is the unique homomorphism sending each generator $g\in U(G)$ to $g\in G$ — i.e. 'reduce words in elements of $G$ using $G$'s own multiplication.' This is the universal property of the free group applied to $\mathrm{id}_{U(G)}$.

hard.q: In $\mathsf{Set}$, the coproduct (categorical colimit) of $\{a,b\}$ and $\{0,1,2\}$ has how many elements?
hard.explain: The coproduct in $\mathsf{Set}$ is the disjoint union, so $|A\sqcup B|=|A|+|B|=2+3=5$.

questions.q: A functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$ is called representable if:
choices: $F$ is naturally isomorphic to $\Hom_{\mathcal{C}}(-,X)$ for some $X\in\mathcal{C}$
questions.explain: Representability means $F\cong h_X$ for some object $X$; by Yoneda this $X$ is unique up to unique iso.

questions.q: The tensor product $M\otimes_R N$ of two $R$-modules represents which functor (on $R$-modules $P$)?
questions.explain: By definition, $M\otimes_R N$ represents $P\mapsto\operatorname{Bilin}_R(M\times N, P)$: every $R$-bilinear map $M\times N\to P$ factors uniquely through the canonical $\otimes\colon M\times N\to M\otimes N$.

questions.q: By Yoneda, how many objects (up to isomorphism) satisfy the universal property of the product $A\times B$ in a given category (assuming at least one exists)?
questions.explain: Representing objects are unique up to unique isomorphism — exactly $1$ isomorphism class.

hard.q: Arrange the canonical steps of a proof that products in a category are unique up to unique isomorphism. Let $P=(A\times B,\pi_1,\pi_2)$ and $Q=(A\times B,\rho_1,\rho_2)$ be two candidate products of the same objects $A$ and $B$.
items: Apply the universal property of $Q$ to the cone $(\pi_1,\pi_2)$ on $P$ to obtain a unique map $u\colon P\to Q$ with $\rho_i\circ u=\pi_i$.
Apply the universal property of $P$ to the cone $(\rho_1,\rho_2)$ on $Q$ to obtain a unique map $v\colon Q\to P$ with $\pi_i\circ v=\rho_i$.
Use uniqueness in the universal property of $P$ to conclude $v\circ u=\mathrm{id}_P$ (both sides are the unique map $P\to P$ compatible with $\pi_1,\pi_2$).
Use uniqueness in the universal property of $Q$ to conclude $u\circ v=\mathrm{id}_Q$ (both sides are the unique map $Q\to Q$ compatible with $\rho_1,\rho_2$).
hard.explain: First produce $u$ and $v$ in either order (A, then B). Then verify $vu=\mathrm{id}_P$ via uniqueness inside $P$'s universal property (C), and $uv=\mathrm{id}_Q$ via uniqueness inside $Q$'s (D). The proof is symmetric between $P$ and $Q$, which is the hallmark of a universal-property argument.

hard.q: Which of the following is a counterexample showing that a left adjoint need not reflect limits?
choices: The free group functor $F\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Grp}$ does not send a product of sets to the product of free groups
The identity functor on $\mathsf{Set}$ preserves all colimits
hard.hint: Which direction (limits vs colimits) does a LEFT adjoint preserve?
hard.explain: Left adjoints preserve colimits but need not preserve — or reflect — limits. The free group functor $F\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Grp}$ sends a two-element product $X\times Y$ to $F(X\times Y)$, the free group on $X\times Y$, which is generally not the product $F(X)\times F(Y)$ in $\mathsf{Grp}$. This is a concrete illustration that LAPC (left adjoints preserve colimits) has no analogue for limits.

hard.q: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be a category with exactly one object $*$ and $\Hom(*,*)=\{\mathrm{id}\}$. How many distinct functors $F\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{C}$ are there?
hard.explain: There is only one object, so $F(*)=*$ is forced. The only morphism $\mathrm{id}_*$ must map to a morphism $*\to*$, i.e. to $\mathrm{id}_*$ itself. So the only functor is the identity — exactly $1$.

questions.q: A monoid object in the monoidal category $(\mathsf{Ab},\otimes_{\mathbb Z},\mathbb Z)$ is:
questions.explain: An abelian group $R$ together with a $\mathbb Z$-bilinear multiplication $R\otimes_{\mathbb Z} R\to R$ (associative) and a unit $\mathbb Z\to R$ is exactly the data of a unital ring.

questions.q: Which coherence diagram must commute in every monoidal category?
choices: Mac Lane's pentagon relating the two associators on a fourfold tensor
questions.explain: Mac Lane's pentagon is one of the two required coherence diagrams (the other is the triangle). The hexagon/naturality of $\sigma$ are extra data for braided/symmetric monoidal categories.

questions.q: How many distinct bracketings of a fourfold tensor product $A\otimes B\otimes C\otimes D$ are there (Catalan number $C_3$)?
questions.explain: The Catalan number $C_3 = 5$ counts full binary trees with $4$ leaves — $((AB)C)D,\ (A(BC))D,\ (AB)(CD),\ A((BC)D),\ A(B(CD))$. Mac Lane's pentagon says all $5$ become equal under $\alpha$.

hard.q: Mac Lane's coherence theorem for monoidal categories states that:
choices: Every monoidal category is equivalent to a strict monoidal category where associativity and unit isomorphisms are identities
The associator $\alpha$ must be the identity morphism
Pentagon and triangle diagrams hold only when all objects are isomorphic
hard.explain: Mac Lane's coherence theorem says every monoidal category is monoidally equivalent to a strict one (where $\otimes$ is strictly associative and $I\otimes A=A=A\otimes I$ on the nose). This justifies treating parenthesizations as equal 'in practice': any diagram built from associators and unit isomorphisms commutes.

hard.q: A commutative monoid object in a symmetric monoidal category $(\mathcal{C},\otimes,I)$ is a monoid $(M,\mu,\eta)$ satisfying additionally $\mu\circ\sigma_{M,M}=\mu$, where $\sigma$ is the symmetry. In $(\mathsf{Ab},\otimes_{\mathbb Z},\mathbb Z)$, what additional structure does this give over an ordinary ring?
hard.explain: Commutativity of the monoid with respect to the symmetry isomorphism $\sigma$ translates exactly to $ab=ba$ in the ring $M$, i.e. $M$ is a commutative ring.

hard.q: The Catalan number $C_n$ counts the bracketings of an $(n{+}1)$-fold tensor. Using $C_n=\binom{2n}{n}/(n+1)$, compute $C_4$ (the number of bracketings of a $5$-fold tensor $A\otimes B\otimes C\otimes D\otimes E$).
hard.explain: $C_4=\binom{8}{4}/5=70/5=14$. These $14$ bracketings all become equal under iterated application of the associator $\alpha$, by Mac Lane's coherence theorem.

questions.q: The Yoneda lemma asserts a bijection $\mathrm{Nat}(h_A,F)\cong F(A)$. The forward map sends a natural transformation $\eta\colon h_A\Rightarrow F$ to:
choices: the component $\eta_A$ regarded as an element of $F(A)$
the natural transformation itself, coerced to a set
questions.explain: Evaluate $\eta$'s $A$-component at the distinguished element $\mathrm{id}_A\in h_A(A)=\Hom(A,A)$. That single value determines every other component via naturality.

questions.q: Select every statement that is an immediate corollary of the Yoneda lemma.
choices: The Yoneda embedding $h_{(-)}\colon\mathcal{C}\hookrightarrow\mathsf{Fun}(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}},\mathsf{Set})$ is fully faithful.
An object is determined up to isomorphism by its functor of points $h_X$.
Representing objects are unique up to unique isomorphism.
questions.explain: Taking $F=h_Y$ gives $\mathrm{Nat}(h_X,h_Y)\cong\Hom(X,Y)$ — full faithfulness. Isomorphism of functors then pulls back to isomorphism of objects. Unique representability is the Yoneda-level uniqueness statement. Not every presheaf is representable (that would be an equivalence, which generally fails).

questions.q: In the poset category on $\{0<1<2\}$ viewed as a category, the representable $h_1=\Hom(-,1)$ satisfies $h_1(C)=\{\ast\}$ iff $C\le 1$, else $\emptyset$. How many natural transformations $h_1\Rightarrow F$ are there for the functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$ with $|F(1)|=4$?
questions.explain: Yoneda: $\mathrm{Nat}(h_1,F)\cong F(1)$, so the count is $|F(1)|=4$.

hard.q: Prove $\mathrm{Nat}(h_A,F)\to F(A)$, $\eta\mapsto\eta_A(\mathrm{id}_A)$, is injective. Pick the correct next step.
steps: Fix any object $C$ and any morphism $\varphi\colon C\to A$.
choices: Apply naturality of $\eta$ and $\eta'$ to $\varphi$ to get $\eta_C(\varphi)=F(\varphi)(x)=\eta'_C(\varphi)$.
Note that $F(\varphi)$ is an isomorphism, hence $\eta_C=\eta'_C$.
Use Yoneda to conclude $\eta=\eta'$ without further computation.
hard.hint: $\varphi=\mathrm{id}_A\circ\varphi$: push it through the naturality square.
hard.explain: Naturality says $\eta_C(\varphi)=F(\varphi)(\eta_A(\mathrm{id}_A))=F(\varphi)(x)$, same for $\eta'$. So components agree on every $C$ and every $\varphi$, giving $\eta=\eta'$.

hard.q: An object $X\in\mathcal{C}$ is called a $\textit{projective generator}$ if $\Hom(X,-)$ is faithful and preserves epimorphisms. By Yoneda, this is equivalent to saying:
choices: $h_X$ is naturally isomorphic to the identity functor.
$h_X\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathsf{Set}$ reflects isomorphisms, and the class $\{X\}$ is 'enough projectives'.
Every object is a quotient of $X$.
hard.explain: Faithfulness of $\Hom(X,-)$ forces $h_X$ to separate morphisms, and preservation of epis under a representable makes $X$ projective. Together they are the standard 'enough projectives' condition.

hard.q: Let $G$ be a finite group and $\mathcal{B}G$ its one-object category. A functor $F\colon\mathcal{B}G^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$ is a right $G$-set. By Yoneda, $\mathrm{Nat}(h_\ast,F)\cong F(\ast)$; for $G=S_3$ and $F(\ast)$ the regular right $S_3$-set, how many natural transformations are there?
hard.explain: $|S_3|=6$, so the regular $S_3$-set has $6$ elements and Yoneda gives $|\mathrm{Nat}(h_\ast,F)|=|F(\ast)|=6$.

questions.q: Match each diagram shape $\mathcal{J}$ with the corresponding limit in $\mathcal{C}$.
questions.explain: Each limit is the universal cone on the corresponding shape: empty → terminal, two-point discrete → product, parallel pair → equalizer, cospan → pullback.

questions.q: In $\mathsf{Set}$, the pullback of $f\colon X\to Z$ and $g\colon Y\to Z$ is:
questions.explain: The pullback is the set of pairs that agree in $Z$ — exactly the universal cone over the cospan.

questions.q: Let $X=\{1,2,3,4\}$, $Y=\{a,b\}$, $Z=\{0,1\}$, with $f\colon X\to Z$ defined $f(1)=f(2)=0$, $f(3)=f(4)=1$, and $g\colon Y\to Z$ defined $g(a)=0$, $g(b)=1$. Compute $|X\times_Z Y|$.
questions.explain: Fiber over $0$: $\{1,2\}\times\{a\}$ gives $2$ pairs; fiber over $1$: $\{3,4\}\times\{b\}$ gives $2$ pairs. Total: $4$.

hard.q: A functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$ $\textit{preserves}$ a limit $L$ of a diagram $D$ if $F(L)$ is a limit of $F\circ D$; it $\textit{reflects}$ limits if every cone $(c_j)$ in $\mathcal{C}$ whose image is a limit cone in $\mathcal{D}$ was already a limit cone. Which statement is false?
choices: Every equivalence of categories preserves and reflects all limits.
hard.hint: A faithful functor can forget just enough structure to reflect isos but not reflect whole limit cones.
hard.explain: Faithful functors reflect isomorphisms but not general limits. E.g. $U\colon\mathsf{Top}\to\mathsf{Set}$ is faithful yet does not reflect products (a non-product topology on $X\times Y$ still has product underlying set).

hard.q: Arrange the steps to show the pullback $X\times_Z Y$ exists in $\mathsf{Set}$.
items: Let $\pi_1\colon P\to X,\pi_2\colon P\to Y$ be the projections; check $f\pi_1=g\pi_2$.
Given any other cone $(h\colon W\to X, k\colon W\to Y)$ with $fh=gk$, define $u\colon W\to P$ by $u(w)=(h(w),k(w))$.
Verify $\pi_1 u=h$, $\pi_2 u=k$, and that $u$ is the unique such map.
hard.explain: Construct the set, equip it with projections, produce the universal map, verify uniqueness.

hard.q: In $\mathsf{Set}$, the coequalizer of $f,g\colon A\rightrightarrows B$ with $A=\{1,2,3\}$, $B=\{x,y,z,w\}$, $f(1)=x,f(2)=y,f(3)=z$ and $g(1)=y,g(2)=z,g(3)=w$ is $B/{\sim}$ where $\sim$ is generated by $f(a)\sim g(a)$. How many elements does the coequalizer have?
hard.explain: The relations $x\sim y$, $y\sim z$, $z\sim w$ collapse all of $B$ to a single equivalence class.

questions.q: $F\dashv G$ means, by definition, that there is an isomorphism, natural in $A$ and $B$:
questions.explain: The defining Hom-isomorphism: mapping out of $F(A)$ in $\mathcal{D}$ is the same data as mapping into $G(B)$ in $\mathcal{C}$.

questions.q: Select every example of an adjunction.
choices: The identity functor $\mathrm{id}\colon\mathsf{Grp}\to\mathsf{Grp}$ is left adjoint to the double-dual $(-)^{**}$
questions.explain: The first three are textbook adjunctions. $(-)^{**}$ on $\mathsf{Grp}$ isn't even functorial in an obvious way — and there's certainly no natural Hom-isomorphism with the identity.

questions.q: For the free–forgetful adjunction $F\dashv U\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Grp}$, compute $|\Hom_{\mathsf{Grp}}(F(\{a\}),\mathbb{Z}/5)|$.
questions.explain: By the Hom-iso, $|\Hom_{\mathsf{Grp}}(F(\{a\}),\mathbb{Z}/5)|=|\Hom_{\mathsf{Set}}(\{a\},\mathbb{Z}/5)|=|\mathbb{Z}/5|=5$.

hard.q: The triangle identities for an adjunction $F\dashv G$ with unit $\eta$ and counit $\varepsilon$ are:
hard.explain: The triangles say 'whisker on one side, then whisker back, and you're home'. Neither $\eta$ nor $\varepsilon$ need be an iso — e.g. $F\dashv U$ for free groups has non-iso $\eta$ and non-iso $\varepsilon$.

hard.q: $F\dashv G$ and $F$ preserves all colimits that exist. Which is the correct slogan?
choices: LAPL / RAPC: left adjoints preserve limits, right adjoints preserve colimits.
LAPC / RAPL: left adjoints preserve colimits, right adjoints preserve limits.
Both adjoints preserve both limits and colimits.
Neither slogan has content: it depends on the category.
hard.hint: Left adjoint to $G$ means 'left side of a hom-iso' — which side of $\Hom$ is covariant in its second argument and commutes with limits?
hard.explain: $\Hom(-,X)$ turns colimits into limits, so $\Hom(F-,-)\cong\Hom(-,G-)$ combined with Yoneda forces $F$ to preserve colimits and $G$ to preserve limits.

hard.q: Let $D\dashv U$ where $D\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Top}$ gives the discrete topology and $U$ is underlying set. For $X=\{0,1\}$ and $Y$ a three-point space with the indiscrete topology, compute $|\Hom_{\mathsf{Top}}(D(X),Y)|$.
hard.explain: By the adjunction, $\Hom_{\mathsf{Top}}(D(X),Y)\cong\Hom_{\mathsf{Set}}(X,U(Y))$ — every function $X\to U(Y)$ is automatically continuous out of a discrete domain. So the count is $|U(Y)|^{|X|}=3^2=9$.

questions.q: A monad on $\mathcal{C}$ is a triple $(T,\mu,\eta)$ where $T\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{C}$ is a functor, $\mu\colon T^2\Rightarrow T$ and $\eta\colon\mathrm{id}\Rightarrow T$ are natural transformations. Which identities must hold?
questions.explain: Associativity of multiplication and the left/right unit laws — the 'monoid-in-endofunctors' axioms.

questions.q: The $\mathrm{Maybe}$ monad on $\mathsf{Set}$ sends $X\mapsto X\sqcup\{\bot\}$. The Kleisli composition $g\circ_K f$ of $f\colon X\to\mathrm{Maybe}\,Y$ and $g\colon Y\to\mathrm{Maybe}\,Z$ is:
choices: undefined without a strict monad structure
questions.explain: Kleisli composition is 'bind then map': unwrap the option from $f$, run $g$ on success, propagate $\bot$ on failure. This is exactly $\mu_Z\circ\mathrm{Maybe}(g)\circ f$.

questions.q: Every adjunction $F\dashv U$ induces a monad $T=UF$ on the source category. For $F\dashv U\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Mon}$ (free monoid / underlying set), what is $|T(\{a\})|$ restricted to words of length $\le 3$ (including the empty word)?
questions.explain: Words in $\{a\}$ of length $0,1,2,3$ are $\varepsilon, a, aa, aaa$ — exactly $4$ elements.

hard.q: A $T$-algebra for a monad $T$ is a pair $(A,\alpha)$ with $\alpha\colon T(A)\to A$ satisfying unit and associativity axioms. For the free-monoid monad $T\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Set}$, a $T$-algebra is:
hard.hint: $T(A)$ is the set of finite words in $A$; $\alpha$ says how to 'multiply' a word.
hard.explain: The structure map $\alpha\colon T(A)\to A$ assigns a product to every finite word; the axioms force this to be associative and unital — i.e. make $A$ a monoid. This is Eilenberg–Moore: algebras for the free-monoid monad = monoids.

hard.q: The Kleisli category $\mathcal{C}_T$ of a monad $T$ on $\mathcal{C}$ has the same objects as $\mathcal{C}$, but $\Hom_{\mathcal{C}_T}(X,Y)=\Hom_{\mathcal{C}}(X,T(Y))$. Which adjunction does $\mathcal{C}_T$ realize?
choices: A right adjoint to the Eilenberg–Moore category with no left adjoint.
The $\textit{initial}$ adjunction generating $T$: $F_T\dashv U_T$ where $F_T\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{C}_T$ sends $X\mapsto X$ and $U_T X=T(X)$.
No adjunction; $\mathcal{C}_T$ is a standalone construction.
The same adjunction as the Eilenberg–Moore category, up to equivalence.
hard.explain: Kleisli is the initial adjunction whose induced monad is $T$ (Eilenberg–Moore is the terminal one). Both recover $T$ as $U_T F_T=T$, but the resulting categories differ: Kleisli is a full subcategory of $\mathcal{C}^T$ on the free algebras.

hard.q: For the list (= free-monoid) monad on $\mathsf{Set}$, Kleisli morphisms $X\to Y$ are functions $X\to\mathrm{List}(Y)$. How many Kleisli morphisms from $\{a\}$ to $\{b\}$ using lists of length exactly $2$ are there?
hard.explain: A function $\{a\}\to\mathrm{List}^{=2}(\{b\})$ picks one list of length $2$ from $\{bb\}$ — exactly $1$ choice.

questions.q: Given functors $F\colon\mathcal{A}\to\mathcal{C}$ and $K\colon\mathcal{A}\to\mathcal{B}$, the $\textit{left Kan extension}$ $\mathrm{Lan}_K F$ is characterized by:
choices: a natural iso $\Hom(\mathrm{Lan}_K F, G)\cong\Hom(F, G\circ K)$ for $G\colon\mathcal{B}\to\mathcal{C}$
questions.explain: The left Kan extension is the left adjoint (up to iso) to precomposition with $K$: $(-)\circ K\colon\mathsf{Fun}(\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C})\to\mathsf{Fun}(\mathcal{A},\mathcal{C})$.

questions.q: Mac Lane's dictum 'all concepts are Kan extensions' is literal: which of the following is NOT an instance of a (co)limit-as-Kan-extension?
choices: A limit of $D\colon\mathcal{J}\to\mathcal{C}$ is the right Kan extension of $D$ along $\mathcal{J}\to\mathbf{1}$.
A colimit of $D$ is the left Kan extension of $D$ along $\mathcal{J}\to\mathbf{1}$.
An adjunction $F\dashv G$ can be expressed via Kan extensions along identities.
Every functor is a Kan extension of itself along itself (tautologically).
questions.hint: A Kan extension along $\mathrm{id}$ is just the functor itself — fine as a tautology. Check whether that counts as a non-trivial 'instance' of a concept.
questions.explain: The tautology is true but vacuous — not a genuine $\textit{instance}$ of a concept being recovered. Limits, colimits, and adjoints really ARE Kan extensions in the substantive sense.

questions.q: Let $K\colon\mathbf{1}\to\mathcal{J}$ be the inclusion of the one-object category into a diagram shape $\mathcal{J}$ with $3$ objects and only identity arrows. For $F\colon\mathbf{1}\to\mathsf{Set}$ picking a $2$-element set, how many objects does $\mathrm{Lan}_K F\colon\mathcal{J}\to\mathsf{Set}$ assign, counted as the sum $\sum_{j\in\mathcal{J}}|\mathrm{Lan}_K F(j)|$?
questions.explain: $K$ hits one of the three objects; $\mathrm{Lan}_K F$ at that object is $F(\ast)$ (size $2$), and the initial (empty) colimit at the other two — so the total is $2+0+0=2$.

hard.q: When $\mathcal{C}$ is cocomplete and $\mathcal{A}$ is small, the pointwise formula $\mathrm{Lan}_K F(b)=\mathrm{colim}\,(K/b\to\mathcal{A}\xrightarrow{F}\mathcal{C})$ says the left Kan extension at $b\in\mathcal{B}$ is:
choices: a limit over the comma category $(K/b)$
a colimit over the comma category $(K/b)$
hard.explain: Left Kan = colimit; right Kan = limit. The pointwise formula identifies the extension with a colimit indexed by the comma category of 'ways $b$ is hit by $K$'.

hard.q: Density theorem (a Kan-extension statement): every presheaf $F\colon\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$ is canonically a colimit of representables. Specifically $F\cong\mathrm{colim}\,(\text{elements}(F)\to\mathcal{C}\xrightarrow{h_{-}}\mathsf{PSh}(\mathcal{C}))$. Which other result does this generalize?
choices: Every vector is a linear combination of basis vectors (only when $\mathcal{C}$ is discrete).
The Yoneda embedding is an equivalence.
hard.hint: What does the density theorem say for $\mathcal{C}=$ a discrete category on a set?
hard.explain: When $\mathcal{C}$ is discrete, a presheaf is just a family of sets, and the colimit of representables recovers the disjoint union — exactly 'expand in the basis of points'. Density is the categorical analogue.

hard.q: For $K\colon\mathcal{A}\to\mathcal{B}$ with $\mathcal{A}$ discrete on $3$ objects, $\mathcal{B}$ discrete on $5$ objects, and $K$ injective on objects with image of size $3$, and $F\colon\mathcal{A}\to\mathsf{Set}$ constantly the $2$-element set, compute $\sum_{b\in\mathcal{B}}|\mathrm{Lan}_K F(b)|$.
hard.explain: At the $3$ objects of $\mathcal{B}$ in the image of $K$, the extension is the $2$-element set; at the other $2$ objects, it's the empty colimit. Total: $3\cdot 2+2\cdot 0=6$.

questions.q: A (strict) 2-category has objects, 1-morphisms between objects, and 2-morphisms between parallel 1-morphisms. Which is the canonical example?
choices: The free category on any graph
Any discrete category on objects alone
questions.explain: $\mathsf{Cat}$ is the archetypal 2-category; the 2-cells are natural transformations and the two composition laws (vertical and horizontal) are the two ways of composing natural transformations.

questions.q: In a 2-category, 2-cells have $\textit{two}$ compositions: vertical (composing $\alpha\colon f\Rightarrow g$ and $\beta\colon g\Rightarrow h$) and horizontal (composing $\alpha\colon f\Rightarrow g$ and $\alpha'\colon f'\Rightarrow g'$ where $f',g'$ are composable with $f,g$). The interchange law says:
choices: vertical and horizontal composition are the same
horizontal composition is strictly associative but vertical is only up to 3-cell
questions.explain: Interchange: the two ways of reading a 'grid' of 2-cells — all-vertical-then-horizontal vs. all-horizontal-then-vertical — agree. This is the defining coherence of a (strict) 2-category.

questions.q: In $\mathsf{Cat}$ viewed as a 2-category, how many 2-cells are there from the identity functor $\mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{C}}$ to itself, when $\mathcal{C}$ is a discrete category on $5$ objects?
questions.explain: A natural transformation $\mathrm{id}\Rightarrow\mathrm{id}$ is a family $\eta_A\colon A\to A$; in a discrete category the only such choice is $\mathrm{id}_A$ at every object. So exactly $1$ 2-cell.

hard.q: A 2-categorical $\textit{adjunction}$ in a 2-category $\mathcal{K}$ consists of 1-morphisms $f\colon A\to B$, $g\colon B\to A$ and 2-cells $\eta\colon\mathrm{id}_A\Rightarrow g\circ f$, $\varepsilon\colon f\circ g\Rightarrow\mathrm{id}_B$ satisfying the triangle equations. In $\mathcal{K}=\mathsf{Cat}$ this specializes to:
choices: an ordinary adjunction $F\dashv G$ of categories
a natural isomorphism between two functors
hard.explain: Exactly: the 2-categorical definition in $\mathsf{Cat}$ reproduces the unit/counit formulation of an ordinary adjunction. 2-category theory is designed so every such notion specializes cleanly.

hard.q: A $\textit{monad in a 2-category}$ $\mathcal{K}$ is an object $A$ together with a 1-morphism $t\colon A\to A$ and 2-cells $\mu\colon tt\Rightarrow t$, $\eta\colon\mathrm{id}_A\Rightarrow t$ satisfying the monad axioms. Specializing to $\mathcal{K}=\mathsf{Cat}$ recovers:
choices: an ordinary monad on a category
hard.explain: A monad on a category $\mathcal{C}$ = a monad in $\mathsf{Cat}$ at the object $\mathcal{C}$. The 2-categorical definition is just the diagrammatic axioms interpreted abstractly.

hard.q: The 2-category $\mathsf{Cat}$ restricted to the full sub-2-category on one object $\mathcal{C}=\mathbf{1}$ (the terminal category) has one object and one 1-morphism (the identity functor). How many 2-cells $\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\Rightarrow\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}$?
hard.explain: The only arrow in $\mathbf{1}$ is $\mathrm{id}_\ast$; the only natural transformation is the one with component $\mathrm{id}_\ast$.

questions.q: Enriched categories replace Hom-SETS by Hom-OBJECTS in a monoidal category $(\mathcal{V},\otimes,I)$. Composition becomes a morphism $\Hom(B,C)\otimes\Hom(A,B)\to\Hom(A,C)$ in $\mathcal{V}$. Identity becomes:
choices: not needed; enriched categories have no identities
questions.explain: The identity 1-morphism at $A$ is a 'point' of the Hom-object, i.e. an arrow out of the monoidal unit $I$.

questions.q: Match each choice of $\mathcal{V}$ with the structure a $\mathcal{V}$-enriched category recovers.
questions.hint: Lawvere (1973) observed that a metric space is precisely a category enriched over the monoidal poset $([0,\infty],\ge)$ with $+$ as tensor: $\Hom(x,y)=d(x,y)$, composition = triangle…
questions.explain: Lawvere (1973) observed that a metric space is precisely a category enriched over the monoidal poset $([0,\infty],\ge)$ with $+$ as tensor: $\Hom(x,y)=d(x,y)$, composition = triangle inequality, identity = $d(x,x)=0$.

questions.q: In a Lawvere metric space (an enriched category over $[0,\infty]$), the 'identity at $A$' is a morphism $0\ge d(A,A)$, i.e. $d(A,A)\le 0$. Combined with $d\ge 0$ this forces $d(A,A)=$ which value?
questions.explain: $0\le d(A,A)\le 0$, so $d(A,A)=0$ — the reflexivity axiom of a pseudo-metric.

hard.q: In Lawvere's enriched-category interpretation, composition $\Hom(B,C)\otimes\Hom(A,B)\to\Hom(A,C)$ becomes the inequality:
hard.hint: The monoidal product in $[0,\infty]$ is $+$, and the hom-order is $\ge$.
hard.explain: A morphism from $x\otimes y$ to $z$ in $([0,\infty],\ge,+)$ is the inequality $x+y\ge z$ — exactly the triangle inequality.

hard.q: A $\mathsf{Set}$-enriched functor is an ordinary functor. A $\mathsf{Ab}$-enriched functor between pre-additive categories is:
choices: a functor whose action on Hom-groups is a group homomorphism
hard.explain: Enriched functoriality = functoriality plus compatibility with the $\mathcal{V}$-structure on Homs. For $\mathcal{V}=\mathsf{Ab}$ this is additivity on hom-groups.

hard.q: A Lawvere metric space with $3$ points $\{A,B,C\}$ satisfies $d(A,B)=2$, $d(B,C)=3$. The enriched-category axiom (triangle inequality) gives the strongest upper bound on $d(A,C)$. What is this bound?
hard.explain: The composition in the enriched category gives $d(A,C)\le d(A,B)+d(B,C)=2+3=5$.
